title: Nested Tab Deep-Linking in Next.js App Router
topic: deep linking patterns for nested secondary tab bars in Next.js 14+ App Router
summary: Path-segment vs query-param vs parallel routes for encoding nested secondary tab state in URLs.
content:
• 1. Overview
  • 2. Path-Segment Encoding
  • 3. Query-Param Encoding
  • 4. Browser History Semantics
  • 5. Layout-Level Persistence
  • 6. Shareable / Bookmarkable Guarantees
  • 7. Common Pitfalls
  • 8. Real-World Examples
  • 9. Decision Matrix
  • 10. Observations for Vector
  • 11. Sources
  • 12. Gaps & Limitations

1. Overview

Vector renders pages such as /work-items with a primary tab bar and a nested secondary tab bar. Today the URL only encodes the primary route; the secondary tab is held in local React state and is therefore lost on hard reload, cannot be bookmarked, and cannot be shared. This paper surveys the two App Router approaches to encoding that secondary tab in the URL — path-segment (e.g. /work-items/work-items) and query-param (e.g. /work-items?tab=work-items) — cross-references the official Next.js 16.x docs against community guidance, and surfaces version-specific pitfalls.

All Next.js documentation citations in this paper are from the App Router docs at nextjs.org/docs/app, version 16.2.4 (lastUpdated 2026-05-05 at the time of this crawl). The hooks and conventions described are stable since Next.js 13.0 (when useRouter moved from next/router to next/navigation), with the main behavioural change being Next.js 14.0's static-rendering bailout for useSearchParams without a <Suspense> boundary. [1][3][7]

2. Path-Segment Encoding

2.1 The shape

A path-segment encoding pushes the secondary tab into the URL as an additional segment: /work-items/work-items, /work-items/portfolio-items, /work-items/initiatives. In App Router this is implemented as a folder under the parent route. Three folder shapes are available: [1][2]

ShapeFolderMatchesNotes
Static segments app/work-items/work-items/page.tsx Only /work-items/work-items One folder per tab; explicit; works with generateStaticParams; routes are individually prerenderable.
Dynamic segment app/work-items/[tab]/page.tsx Any single second segment One file handles every tab; params.tab arrives as a Promise (Next 15+); the page validates the tab against an allow-list and calls notFound() on miss. [2]
Optional catch-all app/work-items/[[...tab]]/page.tsx /work-items AND any sub-segments Lets /work-items render the default tab while /work-items/anything/anywhere still resolves; params.tab is string[] | undefined. [2]

2.2 Minimal example — dynamic segment

// app/work-items/[tab]/page.tsx
import { notFound } from 'next/navigation'

const TABS = ['work-items', 'portfolio-items', 'initiatives'] as const
type Tab = typeof TABS[number]

export default async function Page({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ tab: string }>
}) {
  const { tab } = await params
  if (!TABS.includes(tab as Tab)) notFound()
  return <TabContent tab={tab as Tab} />
}

export function generateStaticParams() {
  return TABS.map((tab) => ({ tab }))
}

2.3 Parallel-route variant — secondary tab as a slot

When the goal is to keep each secondary tab's subtree mounted between switches (preserving scroll and uncommitted form state), the parallel-routes feature provides independent navigation slots. Slots use the @folder convention; they appear as named props on the parent layout and do not add a URL segment by themselves. [3] 

// app/work-items/layout.tsx
export default function Layout({
  children,
  panel,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode
  panel: React.ReactNode
}) {
  return (
    <>
      <PrimaryTabBar />
      <SecondaryTabBar />
      {panel}        {/* @panel slot — tracks its own active subpage */}
      {children}
    </>
  )
}

// app/work-items/@panel/work-items/page.tsx     — tab 1
// app/work-items/@panel/portfolio-items/page.tsx — tab 2
// app/work-items/@panel/default.tsx              — required fallback

Per the Next.js docs: "During client-side navigation, Next.js will perform a partial render, changing the subpage within the slot, while maintaining the other slot's active subpages, even if they don't match the current URL." On hard reload, slots that don't match the URL fall back to default.js — if absent, a 404 is rendered. [3]

Critically, "slots are not route segments and do not affect the URL structure". [3] So a @panel slot does not, on its own, encode the active secondary tab in the URL. Deep-linkability requires combining the slot with a real segment under it (so the slot tracks /work-items/work-items via the slot-internal work-items/page.tsx) or using an intercepting route ((.), (..)) which is the canonical pattern for modal-like overlays rather than full tab swaps. [4]

2.4 Pros, cons, when to use

PropertyStatic / dynamic segmentParallel-route slot
Bookmarkable URLYes — tab is in the path.Only if combined with an actual segment underneath.
Server-renderable on cold loadYes — page.tsx resolves on the request.Yes — but unmatched slots render default.js on hard reload.
Static export (generateStaticParams)Yes for static or dynamic-with-allow-list.Per slot; combinatorial if multiple slots are dynamic.
Layout shared across tabsYes — app/work-items/layout.tsx wraps all tabs and is not re-rendered between siblings. [1]Yes — same layout, plus slots stream independently with their own loading/error UI. [3]
State preserved across switchesLayout-level state survives; page-level state is recreated when the segment changes.Each slot keeps its own active subpage in memory across soft navigations. [3]
Implementation complexityLow — one folder per tab, or one dynamic segment with an allow-list.High — mandatory default.js, catch-all matchers to suppress orphaned slots, and the "all slots at one level must share the same dynamic-vs-prerendered character" constraint. [3]

3. Query-Param Encoding

3.1 The shape

A query-param encoding leaves the path alone and stores the tab in the search string: /work-items?tab=work-items. App Router exposes two read paths and one write path: [5][6]

  • Server Component page: receives searchParams as a Promise prop. "Using searchParams opts your page into dynamic rendering because it requires an incoming request to read the search parameters from." [1]
  • Client Component: useSearchParams() from next/navigation returns a read-only URLSearchParams instance. [5]
  • Write: router.push(pathname + '?' + params.toString()) or router.replace(...) via useRouter(). [6]

3.2 Minimal example — client-side

// app/work-items/SecondaryTabs.tsx
'use client'
import { useRouter, usePathname, useSearchParams } from 'next/navigation'
import { useCallback } from 'react'

const TABS = ['work-items', 'portfolio-items', 'initiatives'] as const

export default function SecondaryTabs() {
  const router = useRouter()
  const pathname = usePathname()
  const searchParams = useSearchParams()
  const active = searchParams.get('tab') ?? 'work-items'

  const onSelect = useCallback((tab: string) => {
    const next = new URLSearchParams(searchParams.toString())
    next.set('tab', tab)
    // replace, not push — tab clicks should not pollute back-button history
    router.replace(`${pathname}?${next.toString()}`, { scroll: false })
  }, [router, pathname, searchParams])

  return (
    <nav>
      {TABS.map((t) => (
        <button key={t} aria-current={t === active} onClick={() => onSelect(t)}>{t}</button>
      ))}
    </nav>
  )
}

3.3 Layouts cannot read searchParams

From the Next.js useSearchParams reference: "Unlike Pages, Layouts (Server Components) do not receive the searchParams prop. This is because a shared layout is not re-rendered during navigation which could lead to stale searchParams between navigations." [5] So if the secondary tab bar is rendered in layout.tsx for design-system reasons (so it stays mounted during children re-renders), it must be split: the bar itself becomes a Client Component that calls useSearchParams, while the layout shell stays a Server Component.

3.4 The nuqs library

nuqs (47ng/nuqs) wraps useSearchParams + useRouter with a useState-shaped API and built-in parsers. Community write-ups from 2025 consistently report it as the path of lowest boilerplate for query-param UIs in Next.js: [8][9][10][11] 

// With nuqs
'use client'
import { useQueryState, parseAsStringLiteral } from 'nuqs'

const TABS = ['work-items', 'portfolio-items', 'initiatives'] as const

export default function SecondaryTabs() {
  const [tab, setTab] = useQueryState(
    'tab',
    parseAsStringLiteral(TABS).withDefault('work-items'),
  )
  // setTab('portfolio-items') updates the URL and re-renders
}

Reported features [8][9][10][11]:

  • Built-in parsers for string-literal unions, integers, booleans, dates, JSON.
  • useQueryStates for managing multiple keys atomically (one URL write).
  • Configurable per-key history: 'push' | 'replace', scroll, shallow, and throttleMs.
  • createSearchParamsCache for type-safe searchParams access in deeply nested Server Components without prop-drilling.
  • Adapter (NuqsAdapter) wraps the root layout; supports App Router and Pages Router, plus React SPA / Remix / TanStack Router.

Status: third-party library, MIT-licensed, no runtime dependencies; the InfoQ writeup for React Advanced 2025 records it as the leading type-safe URL-state library for React. [12] Vector should evaluate it on its own merits, not adopt it on hearsay.

3.5 Pros, cons, when to use

  • Pros. Tab clicks never re-render the route segment, so server components above don't refetch; encoding is orthogonal to the route tree, so adding/removing tabs is a pure data change; multiple state keys can coexist on the same URL (?tab=portfolio-items&sort=updated&dir=desc) without inventing path conventions.
  • Cons. Reading searchParams in a Server Component opts the route into dynamic rendering [1]; reading via useSearchParams forces a Suspense boundary on prerendered routes (see §7); URLs are slightly less aesthetic than path-segment URLs and arguably less SEO-canonical.

4. Browser History Semantics

4.1 push vs replace

Both router.push and router.replace perform a client-side navigation. The difference, from the official API reference [6]:

  • router.push(href, options) — "Adds a new entry into the browser's history stack." Each invocation pushes one back-button hop.
  • router.replace(href, options) — "Perform a client-side navigation to the provided route without adding a new entry into the browser's history stack."

Both accept { scroll: false } to suppress the default scroll-to-top behaviour — relevant for tab clicks where the user expects the page not to jump. [6]

4.2 Industry consensus on tab clicks

The Next.js docs do not legislate which to use for tabs. Community guidance is split [13][14]:

  • Push is the natural default for navigation-y interactions because users expect Back to undo the last meaningful action.
  • Replace is preferred for "view filters" — clicks that change which slice of the same page is shown, not where the user is. The argument is that a user who tabs through five secondary tabs and then clicks Back expects to leave the page, not step back through five intermediate tab states (the "double-back" problem — users press Back, the URL changes but the page doesn't, so they press again).

A frequently-cited compromise: push on the first tab change of a session (so Back returns to wherever the user came from), replace on subsequent tab changes. nuqs exposes per-key history: 'push' | 'replace' so a project can pick once and apply globally. [9][10]

5. Layout-Level Persistence

From the App Router fundamentals: "On navigation, layouts preserve state, remain interactive, and do not rerender." [1] This is the main reason path-segment navigation does not lose scroll or uncommitted form state across tab switches: app/work-items/layout.tsx stays mounted while only the page.tsx below it changes.

Parallel routes generalise this: each @slot is independently streamed with its own loading.tsx and error.tsx, and "Next.js keeps track of the active state (or subpage) for each slot." [3] When the user toggles the secondary tab bar, only the slot subtree re-renders; the rest of the page is unaffected.

Query-param navigation also preserves the layout (because the route segment doesn't change), but it re-renders the page component on every searchParams change — a community caveat that comes up repeatedly: "When switching tabs, the entire page can re-render, causing unaffected parallel routes to make requests again, when the goal is to only re-render the specific tab slot." [13] In practice this means: if the secondary tab's panel does its own data fetching, query- param flips re-fetch unless the data layer is cached or the panel is wrapped in memo/Suspense.

6. Shareable / Bookmarkable Guarantees

A URL is "truly deep-linkable" only if all of the following hold:

  1. Server-renderable on cold load. Pasting the URL into a fresh browser tab must produce the right page without needing client JS to interpret a fragment or local state. Both path-segment and query-param encodings satisfy this; parallel-route slots satisfy it only via default.js fallbacks on hard navigation. [3]
  2. No hydration flash. If the prerendered HTML shows the "Work Items" tab selected and then client JS swaps to "Portfolio Items" because useSearchParams only resolves after hydration, the user sees a flash. The Next.js fix is to wrap the tab bar in <Suspense> with a fallback that matches the eventual content, or to use the page's searchParams prop and pass the value down. [5]
  3. No auth-redirect loops. Middleware that redirects unauthenticated users should preserve the full URL (path + query) in a ?from= parameter, so post-login the user lands back on the exact tab.
  4. Search-engine indexable. Both forms are indexable. Path segments are widely considered more SEO-friendly because each tab is a distinct URL with its own canonical; query-string variants risk being treated as duplicates of the base URL unless explicit <link rel="canonical"> hints are emitted.
  5. Stable across version upgrades. Path segments are stable; query keys are easy to rename (with backwards-compat shims).

7. Common Pitfalls

7.1 Missing Suspense boundary with useSearchParams

Since Next.js 14.0, calling useSearchParams in a prerendered route without a <Suspense> boundary fails the production build with the missing-suspense-with-csr-bailout error: [7][15][16]

From the Next.js docs: "During production builds, a static page that calls useSearchParams from a Client Component must be wrapped in a Suspense boundary, otherwise the build fails... If a route is prerendered, calling useSearchParams will cause the Client Component tree up to the closest Suspense boundary to be client-side rendered." [5]

The dev server hides this because routes are rendered on demand. Three remediations: [5][7]

  • Wrap the smallest subtree calling useSearchParams in <Suspense>. The static shell still prerenders; the searchParams- dependent tree client-renders.
  • Force the route dynamic via await connection() in the page (replaces the deprecated export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic').
  • Read searchParams in the Server Component page and pass values down as props; this opts into dynamic rendering but eliminates the hook entirely. [1][5]

Debugging tip from Next.js: next build --debug-prerender emits stack traces locating the offending hook call. [16]

7.2 Layouts cannot receive searchParams

Already noted in §3.3 — the layout doesn't re-render on query changes, so the Next team disabled the prop to prevent stale-data bugs. [5] If the secondary tab bar lives in a layout, only its Client Component child can read the active tab.

7.3 params is now a Promise

Since Next.js 15, params on dynamic routes is a Promise — await params in Server Components, use(params) in Client Components. "In version 14 and earlier, params was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future." [2] Type the page with PageProps<'/work-items/[tab]'> for inferred typing.

7.4 Parallel-route landmines

  • default.js is mandatory. Without it, hard refresh on a state where one slot doesn't match the URL renders 404 instead of the page. [3]
  • Slots that should hide on certain routes need a catch-all sink. The docs recommend @slot/[...catchAll]/page.tsx returning null to suppress the slot when navigating to a path it shouldn't appear on. [3]
  • Mixed prerender/dynamic at one level is not allowed. "If one slot is dynamic, all slots at that level must be dynamic." [3]
  • Catch-all params shadow the slot. Combining parallel routes with dynamic catch-all segments has a known interaction bug history (see vercel/next.js discussion #48340) — test the exact combination before committing. [17]

7.5 Tabs disappear on hard refresh

Specific to parallel routes: if the secondary tab bar is rendered inside a @panel slot (rather than the outer layout), and the user hard-refreshes on a URL the slot doesn't match, default.js renders — if it returns null the bar vanishes. Render the tab bar in the layout shell (which always mounts) and let the slot render only the panel content. [3]

8. Real-World Examples

This section is restricted to URL patterns that are directly observable in the product's public address bar. No internal architectural claims are made.

ProductEncoding observedExample URLNotes
GitHub repo tabs Path-segment /owner/repo/issues, /owner/repo/pulls, /owner/repo/actions Each tab is its own URL segment under the repo root; sub-tabs (e.g. issue filter state) move into query params on the same segment.
GitHub Issues filter state Query-param (within the segment) /owner/repo/issues?q=is%3Aopen+sort%3Aupdated-desc Demonstrates the layered convention: tab = path, in-tab state = query.
Vercel dashboard project tabs Path-segment /team/project/deployments, /team/project/analytics, /team/project/settings Built on Next.js itself; the team appears to have standardised on path-segment for nav-level tabs.
Linear issue triage tabs Path-segment /{workspace}/team/{key}/active, /{workspace}/team/{key}/backlog Triage states are URL segments under the team root (verifiable from the Linear web app).

Pattern across observed products: primary navigation tabs are path-segment; in-tab refinements (filter, sort, search, page number) are query-param. Tab clicks at the navigation tier replace history (Back leaves the section, not the tab); in-tab refinements typically replace as well, so Back undoes the entire visit rather than each filter tweak.

9. Decision Matrix

Criterion Path-segment /work-items/work-items Query-param /work-items?tab=work-items Parallel-route slot
Bookmarkable Yes (canonical URL) Yes (URL includes query) Only when paired with a real segment under the slot
Server-renderable on cold load Yes Yes (route is dynamic) Yes via default.js
SEO-friendly (distinct canonical) Strong — one URL per tab Weak — risk of duplicate-content unless canonicalised Strong (if backed by real segments)
Static export possible Yes (allow-list generateStaticParams) No — searchParams forces dynamic rendering [1] Per slot; mixed prerender/dynamic at one level forbidden [3]
Implementation complexity Low Low (medium with raw useSearchParams; low with nuqs) High — default.js, catch-all sinks, slot-uniformity rule
Composes with other URL state Awkward — secondary state needs its own segments or query atop Native — just add another key Awkward — each new state is another slot
Layout re-render on tab change No (segment changes; layout stays) [1] No (route segment unchanged) No (slot streams independently) [3]
Page re-render on tab change Yes — new page module loads Yes — same page, new searchParams Only the slot subtree
Plays well with middleware auth Yes — full URL is preserved Yes — query carried through redirects when handled correctly Yes (slot resolves after auth)
History cleanliness (push) Each tab is a back-button stop — user-controlled via <Link> vs router.replace Same — user picks push or replace per click Same — navigation API decides
Parameter rename cost High — folder rename + redirects Low — rename key + alias old key in handler High — folder rename + slot wiring
Deep-linkability score (subjective) 9 / 10 8 / 10 7 / 10 (depends on slot layering)

10. Observations for Vector

Stated as observations — the architectural call sits with the Vector team.

  • The two real candidates are static path-segment and query-param. Parallel routes are powerful but the docs flag — correctly — that they are aimed at "highly dynamic sections such as dashboards and feeds" with independently- streaming subtrees [3]. A nested secondary tab bar over the same page is a thinner use case, and the slot machinery (default.js, catch-all sinks, slot- uniformity rule) adds a maintenance surface that does not seem to pay for itself unless Vector wants per-tab independent error/loading UI.
  • Path-segment matches the convention of every product surveyed. GitHub, Linear, and the Vercel dashboard all encode primary in-page tabs as path segments and reserve query strings for in-tab refinements. Picking path-segment aligns Vector with the user expectation those products have established.
  • Query-param scales better when tabs are themselves user-data. If the secondary tabs eventually become tenant-defined (e.g. portfolio layers, which Vector already treats as up to ten tenant-built layers per project_portfolio_layers_independent.md), encoding them as path segments under a tenant-known allow-list grows complex; a single ?tab=<slug> with server-side validation is more flexible.
  • If query-param is chosen, evaluate nuqs. The boilerplate to compose useSearchParams + useRouter + manual URLSearchParams is non-trivial, especially once multiple state keys coexist (sort, filter, page, etc.). nuqs compresses that into a useState-shaped API with type-safe parsers and per-key history mode. [8][9][10][11][12]
  • Use router.replace({ scroll: false }) for tab clicks. Tab clicks at the secondary level are filter-shaped: users do not expect Back to step through their tab history. Push reserved for genuine navigation (primary nav, drill-down).
  • If useSearchParams is introduced, audit prerender boundaries. Every Client Component that calls the hook must be reachable through a <Suspense> boundary or the route must be explicitly marked dynamic via connection(). A failed audit is a build break, not a runtime warning. [5][7][16]
  • Either way, render the tab bar in the layout, not inside the panel. Layouts preserve mount across navigation [1]; rendering the bar at the panel level re-mounts the bar on every tab switch (path-segment) or risks stale focus state (query-param).

12. Gaps & Limitations

  • The crawled Next.js docs page reports version 16.2.4 with lastUpdated 2026-05-05. Behaviour described here is current at crawl time; older Next 14.0 / 14.1 behaviour around the missing-suspense-with-csr-bailout bailout was paraphrased from the permanent error reference page rather than the per-version migration notes — if Vector targets a specific Next.js minor, re-verify the build behaviour against that minor's release notes.
  • Vercel-dashboard, Linear, and GitHub URL patterns are reported only from publicly observable URLs. No internal architectural claims (whether they use parallel routes, query params, or custom routers) are made — only the externally visible pattern.
  • The web-search query for Linear / Vercel / GitHub URL conventions returned thin results; the patterns recorded in §8 are based on direct observation of those products' public web apps rather than vendor documentation. A team member with access to those apps should spot-check before this paper is cited as authority.
  • Performance characteristics (e.g. exact bundle-size impact of nuqs, measured render counts under each encoding) were not benchmarked — community claims of "extremely small" are not substantiated with numbers in the sources consulted.
  • The interaction between generateStaticParams on a dynamic-segment tab route and Vector's tenant-aware permission gates was not investigated — if tabs are conditionally available per role, static export of all tab variants may leak the existence of a hidden tab to anonymous users.
  • Server-Components-with-the-searchParams-prop pattern (an alternative to the useSearchParams client hook) was noted but not deeply explored; it is the most SSR-friendly read path for query-param tabs and may merit its own follow-up paper if Vector adopts the query-param encoding.
